review_text: (Episode 5 of the IMDb top 100) This film is absolutely brilliant. Such incredible acting, writing, and cinematography. Just a brilliant film through and through!

An excellent courtroom drama, with an end-of-film twist I didn't see coming, though a bit too melodramatic towards the end for me. Charles Laughton is worth the price of admission here, as a cantankerous senior defense lawyer.
Among the greatest recommend for everyone
Charles Laughton excels as the estimable SIr Wilfrid, as does Marlene Dietrich, in this 50s classic. Don't miss it.

A Classic That Still Packs a Punch, Even If You See the Twists Coming: Billy Wilder’s Witness for the Prosecution is a masterclass in suspense, wit, and acting. Even after all these years, the film’s tension and craftsmanship hold up remarkably well. Charles Laughton is a force of nature as Sir Wilfrid Robarts, chewing scenery with relish, while Marlene Dietrich’s icy, enigmatic Christine Vole steals every scene she’s in. The non-linear storytelling and Wilder’s razor-sharp direction keep you on the edge of your seat, even if the twists, once groundbreaking, now feel familiar after decades of imitators. The black-and-white cinematography and period details are immaculate, and the courtroom scenes crackle with energy. My only quibble? The twists, while brilliantly executed, have been copied so often that their impact is slightly dulled for first-time viewers today. Still, the acting and direction are top-notch, and the emotional payoff lands beautifully. 7/10: a must-watch for fans of classic cinema, even if it’s not quite the revelation it once was.

I'm actually seeing this onstage later this evening and wanted to become acquainted with this Agatha Christie story before then. What I didn't expect was a full on alchemic triumph of effortless story execution, pinpoint humour and mystery until the very end, Witness for the Prosecution is all parry and punch from the moment it begins, crackling with emotional electricity. Billy Wilder skilfully skirts the guidelines of the Hayes Code to deliver plenty of biting humour in his dialogue, lightening the mood with amusing consistency, punctuating the accumulating evidence weighing against the defendant. What's incredible is that despite having no British personnel among the film's principal creative team and only one British name in its trio of stars, the film feels remarkably British for a 1950s American adaptation. Backed by an incredible set of performances largely from Charles Laughton who takes what could be a hammy role, the brilliant legal mind, theatrical courtroom performer, and wily curmudgeon, and invests it with a sense of passion for his work and a sense of duty and honour beyond winning. The double act energy he shares with Elsa Lanchester, absolutely makes the film. Absolutely gripping from beginning to end, I can’t wait for tonight.

May seem like your ordinary courtroom drama except it’s not when remains truly one of cinema’s best case of unpredictability as the accumulating impact generally surprises, attributing to performative heft’s captivating showcases in differential degrees of satisfaction, solely by stellar execution and noted exhaustion. With little compelled exposure, this is certainly an adapted testament to Agatha Christie’s engaging literature that distinctively sweeps. (A-)

I sat through this film thinking it seemed like a pretty ordinary but fun murder mystery, wondering why it was ranked 64th greatest movie of all time. Don't worry. By the time the ending credits roll, you will know why this film stands out as one of the greatest films of all time. Charles Laughton -I'd heard the name before- now I can put an acting performance to the name- is great. And Marlene Dietrich adds an iconic performance you will never forget. She was the inspiration for Blazing Saddles's Lili Von Shtüpp.
Classic Billy Wilder film with great performances from Charles Laughton and Marlene Dietrich
Muy buena! Buenos diálogos, buen concepto, buenas actuaciones, y el plot twist más plot twist que he visto en años! Me encantó.
What a beautiful classic movie. Recommended to anyone who loves good cinema.
Watching this film quite recently reminded me of how slowly a story was told in by-gone times. I thoroughly enjoyed watching this one for a second time, after more than forty years.
A gripping story, terrific performances, and superb twists combine to make Witness for the Prosecution a classic courtroom thriller, and an arguably perfect film.
I'm 33 minutes in and I think he did it

A great courtroom drama. There were a few twists. Sir Wilfred was hilarious. It was British, so the lawyers wore these wigs. It was directed by Billy Wilder. Also this used to be an Agatha Christie play.
Carried all the way by the Laughton performance. The story is a fairly tight courtroom drama with enough classic twists and turns in its final act to wrap it all up very nicely.

Sir Charles rallies from an alcoholic stupor to deliver a fine performance at a time when his career was in decline. It's another "Hitchcockian" film not directed by Sir Alfred though it's commonly believed that he did. Miss Dietrich and Tyrone Power are good as the scheming couple. Director Billy Wilder is better known for films like "Some Like It Hot" but of his films that could be compared to this one, Stalag 17 comes to mind.
The first half annoyed me with the long flashback scenes. Once they moved on to trying the case in court, it got better and better.
Charles Laughton and Tyrone Powers stole the show.....well done....cool twist at the end that I didnt really see coming....well acted, directed, composed. Highly recommend this 50s classic.

The director of this courtroom drama about a mystery crime was Billy Wilder. Charles Laughton, Tyrone Power, and Marlene Dietrich are the main stars in the film. The film is based on Agatha Christie's 1953 play of the same name. The movie was nominated for several Academy Awards. Set at London's Old Bailey, Sir Wilfrid (Charles) is a senior barrister who has recently been discharged from hospital owing to health concerns and is accompanied by a nurse to care for his health and administer medication on time. Wilfrid's doctor has barred him from taking on cases, particularly murder cases that might have an impact on his health. However, he quickly takes on the case of defending a former war veteran who is suspected of murdering an old woman he had been meeting and is claimed to have received a large sum of money in a recently found bequest. The film employs bleak humorous dialogues, largely delivered by Charles Laughton. The plot features fantastic legal debate and is perhaps one of the legal movies I've seen in which the whole film is exclusively around the defense analyzing the complexities of the case and how to handle it. Charles Laughton is the only driving force in the film, making it worthwhile to see. Billy Wilder handles Agatha Christie's play in a satisfactory manner, keeping the mystery alive all the way until the finale, which gives three victories and one victory that lives very briefly.
A rare movie that has twists and turns ably done by good actors. It is entertaining and enlightening as well.

Watchable and a opportunity to see the great Dietrich and Charles Laughton. I was never a big fan of Agatha Christie preferring Raymond Chandler when discussing crime. If you like this more polished presentation it is a movie that you will enjoy. An interesting piece of gossip is that Laughton and Tyrone Power had a more personal relationship than the one depicted here. Again, we have an unrealistic instant stabbing death. Unless hitting the heart itself, a single stabbing wound does not result in instant death.
Legendary at times. Some styles are primitive, only natural for the time.

In court, everyone has something that contradicts what's true and what isn't! As an aging lawyer takes on a client accused of a murder, he starts to suspect that there are much deeper secrets that both the defendant and the the defendants wife are hiding. A twisted thriller that only Agatha Christie can come up with, this legal mystery has us on the edge of our toes!
A delight from the opening credits to the closing. Charles Laughton at the height of his skills and supported by a stellar cast. It's a shame they don't make them like this anymore.
Keeping the crisp dialogue and plot machinations from Agatha Christie's seminal play, Billy Wilder's Witness for the Prosecution remains a tentpole courtroom drama.
Dated, yes, at times hammy, certainly. But awesome performances from both Laughton and Dietrich. Very enjoyable overall.
One of the best Legal Thrillers in the history of the cinema
I love trial movies and unreliable narrators. Movie delivers well on both aspects Marlene shining bright as always. Very good adaptation.
Perfect movie! Impeccable writing, twists and acting.
Clever clever plot, great cast, well done --- with a few eye twinkles, too.
A worthy adaptation of Agatha Christie's outstanding stage drama, Witness For the Prosecution is a fantastic film that boasts a few powerhouse performances.

Movie is an entertaining courtroom drama and murder mystery with lots of comedic banter. Stellar performances from Tyrone Power, Marlene Dietrich, and especially Charles Laughton, who commands this film with his sharp wit. I admit, alot of the twists and turns I did not see coming, which added to the viewing fun

Set and directed as though for theatre, but clearly produced as a cinematic experience. It's filled with wit, bombast, animated dialogue, and some clever repartee. The exaggerated performances are typical of the period, with the added emotion really supporting any tension and plot twists
Charles Laughton was absolutely superb...as was the plot twist.

I just happened to watch this on the exact same day the murder in this movie took place (Friday October 14th) , and I was truly blown away by the acting (from all of the cast) and the plot. The multiple twists that this movie takes at the end truly made this movie special and I never thought that I would end up loving it! This is one of the better black and white films that I have seen or films made from this time period and I would highly recommend that anyone reading this check this movie out on Freevee! You will not regret it at all!

This is the worst Agatha Christie mystery I have ever seen. The essence of Christie is to fill the play with subtle clues and let the audience sort them out. The essence of this play is total misdirection: to have the defendant and chief witnesses appear to be exactly the opposite of who they really are. Also, important circumstantial evidence is totally inconsistent with guilt. For example, the window broken from the outside. The defendant wouldn't have broken the window from the outside because he was a friend of the murdered woman. The woman's assistant was not a credible witness because she was hard-of-hearing and had a financial motive to lie. The letters were not credible because they would have been mailed to Germany, And, the trial itself was preposterous. Lawyers are not allowed to yell at and argue with witnesses like that.

Out of the many Billy Wilder films I've seen, this one is now counted highly among my favorites for it yet again features the crisp and cutting dialogue and memorable characters that the director is known for. Of course, the ever-dominating Charles Laughton takes centerstage, bringing out not just the terse, piercing, piggish, rudeness of Robarts, but also the character's witty humor and ultimate good nature. Marlene Dietrich, as well, astounds as the volatile and cold Christine, imbuing each scene with magnetism and what could just be called simple awe.

A fun and creative Agatha Christie whodunnit with the added twists of the procedural investigation being conducted in a courtroon rather than with the estimable Hercule Poirot in some exotic locale. In his place we have Charles Laughton, who tosses every trope you might think of for a stuffy English barrister - fussy, detail-obsessed, clever, fastidious, intolerable, and brimming with dry humor - a brings them to life in Sir Wilfrid Robarts. Pitted against him is the mysterious Christine Vole, played with equal capability by the great Marlene Dietrich, whose motives and role in the murder remain fluid and undecided until the film's final moments. Perhaps I let this particular film hang around a bit too long in the list of world classics of cinema; I walked away having expected slightly more than what I got. The performances were very nice (particularly Dietrich's puzzling persona and Laughton's charming curmudgeon), but the plot itself swings around in fits and starts, with a humorous introduction to Sir Wilfrid's character followed by protracted periods of flashbacks that serves mostly as context for the more engaging courtroon scenes and the final twist reveal. Still, it all comes together as material to flesh out each of the individual characters and provide more of a justification for the dueling of personalities rather than dry fact, and makes for a very entertaining film regardless. It is always great to see Laughton bicker with his real-life wife, Elsa Lanchester, in some of their shared films; their chemistry is undeniable and translates so well to the screen. (3.5/5)
Fabulous movie. Full of suspense. Great twist. Amazing performances by Elsa Lanchester and Charles Laughton.

Billy Wilder, Agatha Christie and (trailing a bit behind them because this isn't really his movie, it just seems that way) Alfred Hitchcock walk into a bar... Charles Laughton--turning in one of his finest performances--plays an ornery, acid-tongued, bulldog London barrister hired to defend a frightened Tyrone Power against charges of murdering a ditzy old dowager for her dough. Twists, turns and more than a helping of humor, supplied mostly by the comically contentious relationship between Laughton and his supremely annoying nurse, Elsa Lancaster (Laughton's real-life wife). With the great Marlene Dietrich as Powers' loyal (and oh-so-tricky) spouse, and a multi-whammy ending that is one of Christie's best. Based on a play by Dame Agatha. Worth seeing--or seeing again--at any time. A classic.
Courtroom classic with a bit of a predictable twist at the end but followed by a few more unexpected twists. Some great characters, slightly hammy but fun to watch.
Did not expect what came to be by the end. Unforeseeable!
They don't make movies like this any more. Brilliant plot, brilliant acting. Yes it's in monochrome, but that's totally unimportant.

Charles Laughton and Marlene Dietrich in top form ably supported by Elsa Lanchester. Tyrone Power seemed a bit out of his depth amongst this acting talent. Engaging story full of wit and energy - not a spare moment in the film. The courtroom seemed a bit too grand even for the Old Bailey. The lovely old fashioned buffet at Euston station - so much better than what we have these days.

Enjoyed watching one I hadn't seen that was this good. Loved the ending. These classic actors made it even better. Sadly, this was Tyrone Power's last film. At age 44, he died of a heart attack not long after. Marlene Dietrich was perfect in this role.
This is a great combination of the story writing of Agatha Christie, the directing powers of Billy Wilder, and the acting powers of Marlene Dietrich. The result is fabulous.
Excellent courtroom drama with great performances by Laughton and Dietrich.
it was very entertaining movie from start to finish,
